She has had scalp tenderness — she describes brushing her hair in the mornings as 'the scalp rejecting the comb.' Aching in both shoulders and hips for the past month — she says her body has been 'rearranging itself.' Weight loss of approximately 3kg over one month, unintentional. Fatigue and general malaise. One episode last week of brief pain behind the left eye — she mentioned this as an afterthought, then changed the subject.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "The head has been going on for weeks. The shoulders, yes, old news. The weight, I noticed. (waves hand) And the eye. Once. Last week. (firmly) That one was different. I am not going to say more about the eye."
pe_findings:
Patient permitted the following only:
— Left temporal artery: tender on palpation, nodular, markedly reduced pulsation. She stopped mid-sentence when this was palpated.
— Scalp tenderness over left temporal region.

(Fundoscopy, cranial nerve assessment, auscultation, and full neurological examination declined.)
imaging: CT head without contrast: no intracranial haemorrhage, no mass lesion, no midline shift. No aneurysm identified on non-contrast study. No territorial infarct. (Patient agreed to CT only after being told the machine 'reads without touching.')

special_test.name: ESR + CRP + CBC
special_test.result: ESR: 98 mm/hr (markedly elevated — normal <20). CRP: 64 mg/L (markedly elevated — normal <5). CBC: normocytic anaemia, Hgb 10.8. WBC and platelets normal. Pattern consistent with significant systemic inflammatory process.

memory_fragments.pe: She stopped mid-sentence when the temporal artery was palpated. She did not finish what she was saying. She did not start a new sentence for eleven seconds.
memory_fragments.imaging: She has been touching the left side of her face for three weeks. She thought it was something she was meant to feel. She was right about that part.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Hgb 10.8 (mild normocytic anaemia — anaemia of inflammation). WBC 9.2. Plt 420 (mildly elevated — reactive thrombocytosis). Consistent with systemic inflammatory process.
tests.pt_remark: Oh that one is high. I knew that one was high. Write down that I told you first.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Creatinine 0.9, sodium 138, glucose 96. LFTs normal. No metabolic abnormality.
tests.pt_remark: Normal, of course. That is not where the answer is. I could have saved you the blood.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm rate 78. No ischaemic changes. Not indicated for this presentation.
tests.pt_remark: The heart is fine. Perfectly good heart. Wasted on the rest of me some days but fine.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. Not indicated.
tests.pt_remark: The heart is fine, I already told you. Are you writing this down?

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.
tests.pt_remark: (distracted, looking at something on the wall) Mm.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated for this presentation.
tests.pt_remark: I drew the line at blood. That one I drew a different line.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Not obtained. Patient declined. 'That one requires something I have not agreed to give.'
tests.pt_remark: No. (cheerfully) Absolutely not.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR 1.0, PTT 29. Normal. Relevant given family history of intracranial haemorrhage — coagulopathy excluded.
tests.pt_remark: My father's blood. His problem entirely. Not hereditary. I checked.

hints: New unilateral temporal headache in a patient over 50. Scalp tenderness on the same side. Shoulder and hip girdle aching. Weight loss. Fatigue. One episode of pain behind the left eye, mentioned briefly and then deflected.
ESR 98. CRP 64. Normocytic anaemia. Reactive thrombocytosis. A markedly elevated inflammatory response in a 67-year-old with constitutional symptoms and unilateral temporal findings.
The eye symptom was mentioned as an afterthought. That is frequently how the most important symptom presents. Pain behind one eye, briefly, once — in this context.
Temporal artery biopsy confirms — but treatment must not wait for it. One episode of ocular involvement is enough. The window before irreversible vision loss may already be closing.

management.title: Initial approach — patient is refusing examination
management.hint: She has refused most of the history and examination. She has agreed to stay. How do you approach her?

options.text: "I hear that you already know. I am not here to take that from you. My only role is to make sure nothing dangerous is happening. Would you allow me to check just a few things — for safety, not for doubt."
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Confronting her belief system directly produces resistance. Validating without agreeing, framing the examination as safety-focused rather than diagnostic, and asking permission rather than asserting authority are the evidence-based approaches to patients with strongly held alternative belief frameworks.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Permission first. Always permission first."
options.pt_remark_correct: (tilts head. studies you.) You are not trying to change it. Good. Then we can proceed.

options.text: "I understand that is how you feel. However there is a risk this could be dangerous and I need to examine you. Please cooperate so we can proceed."
options.feedback_wrong: Confronting a patient's belief system directly — especially one this entrenched — produces immediate and total resistance. She will leave. The temporal artery will remain unexamined.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (stands immediately) Dangerous. Yes yes. (picks up her bag) You sound like my daughter.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "She was not asking to be believed. She was asking not to be dismissed."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "She left. The ESR was 98. The visual symptoms had already started. You corrected her beliefs instead of examining her temporal artery."

options.text: "Your safety takes priority here. I am going to proceed with the examination now."
options.feedback_wrong: A competent adult has the right to refuse examination. Proceeding without consent is assault regardless of clinical urgency. The correct approach is to persuade, not to override.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (pulls back sharply) I said no. I was very clear about no.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Competent adult. Refused consent. You proceeded anyway."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "She had capacity. She refused. You proceeded without consent. Whatever the diagnosis — this is how you lost her."

options.text: "It sounds like you have a strong sense of what is happening. Could you tell me more about what you believe this is, and why you feel that way?"
options.feedback_wrong: Exploring her belief system first delays the safety assessment and does not move her toward allowing examination. Validation of her perspective combined with a safety frame and explicit permission request is more effective.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (settles back with visible pleasure) Oh good. Finally. (begins at length) It started with a dream in the third week of —
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "She will talk for a long time. The artery will not wait."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "You sent her away. ESR 98. Transient visual loss. She lost the eye within 48 hours."

management.title: Giant Cell Arteritis is confirmed. What does the temporal artery biopsy show?
management.hint: The biopsy has returned. Which histological finding confirms Giant Cell Arteritis?

options.text: Granulomatous inflammation of the media with multinucleated giant cells and fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina
options.feedback_correct: Correct. GCA shows granulomatous inflammation within the vessel wall — predominantly in the media — with multinucleated giant cells, infiltration by lymphocytes and macrophages, and disruption/fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina. Intimal hyperplasia causes luminal narrowing and ischaemia.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Giant cells cluster at the internal elastic lamina. That is where the destruction begins."
options.pt_remark_correct: (does not look at the report) I knew before the needle. But thank you for confirming.

options.text: Homogeneous acellular thickening of the arteriolar wall with hyaline deposition
options.feedback_wrong: Hyaline arteriolosclerosis — homogeneous acellular wall thickening with hyaline deposition — is the pattern of chronic hypertension and diabetes in small vessels. It does not produce granulomatous inflammation or giant cells.
options.pt_remark_wrong: That is not it. (with confidence)
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Hyaline is chronic pressure damage. Acellular. No inflammation at all."

options.text: Concentric onion-skin layering of the arteriolar wall with intimal proliferation
options.feedback_wrong: Concentric onion-skin intimal proliferation is hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis — seen in malignant hypertension. Not the pattern of giant cell arteritis.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Also not it. (same confidence)
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Onion-skin is malignant hypertension — concentric, not granulomatous."

options.text: Transmural inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis of the vessel wall
options.feedback_wrong: Fibrinoid necrosis with transmural inflammation is the pattern of polyarteritis nodosa — a necrotising vasculitis affecting medium vessels. GCA is granulomatous, not necrotising. Giant cells are absent in PAN.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Close. But close is not the same thing.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "PAN necrotises. GCA granulates. Giant cells are absent in PAN."

management.title: Immediate treatment — before biopsy
management.hint: GCA confirmed clinically. Transient visual loss has already occurred. What do you do first?

options.text: High-dose IV methylprednisolone immediately — do not wait for temporal artery biopsy
options.feedback_correct: Correct. In GCA with visual symptoms, steroid treatment must not wait for biopsy confirmation. The window before irreversible ischaemic optic neuropathy is narrow. High-dose corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone 500-1000mg for 3 days in vision-threatened GCA) should be started immediately. Biopsy remains valid up to 2 weeks after steroid initiation.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Ophthalmic artery is a branch of the internal carotid. Not the external. That is why it is at risk."
options.pt_remark_correct: (looks at the syringe) The cards kept pointing to a needle. I kept hoping it meant something else. The eye. You are saying this changes what is coming.

options.text: Wait for temporal artery biopsy result before starting steroids
options.feedback_wrong: Waiting for biopsy before treating vision-threatened GCA risks irreversible visual loss. Biopsy remains diagnostic for up to 2 weeks after steroid initiation — the diagnosis is not lost by treating first. Vision may be.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More waiting. (sighs) The eye went a little dark again this morning. I did not mention that earlier.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "The biopsy does not expire. The vision does."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "You waited for the biopsy. She lost the eye before the result came back. The biopsy was still positive."

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 40mg — standard GCA dose
options.feedback_wrong: Oral prednisolone 40-60mg is appropriate for GCA without visual involvement. Vision-threatened GCA requires IV methylprednisolone (500-1000mg daily for 3 days) for maximum anti-inflammatory effect before stepping down to oral.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A tablet. (looks at it) The cards showed a needle. This is not a needle.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "IV achieves serum levels oral cannot. The ophthalmic artery does not wait for absorption."

options.text: Refer to ophthalmology and await their guidance before treating
options.feedback_wrong: Ophthalmology should be involved — but treatment should not wait for their review. In vision-threatened GCA, every hour matters. Start steroids, then refer.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Another one who wants to look at my eyes. (to the ceiling) Why does everyone want the eyes.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Treat then refer. The ophthalmic artery does not wait for a consult."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "You waited for ophthalmology before treating. The referral took four hours. The window had already closed."

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Steroids started, biopsy arranged, vision-threatened GCA. Where does she go?

options.text: Admit for IV methylprednisolone course, ophthalmology review, rheumatology referral
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Vision-threatened GCA requires inpatient admission for IV steroid course (3 days), ophthalmology review, and rheumatology involvement for long-term management planning. Temporal artery biopsy arranged as inpatient or urgent outpatient within 2 weeks.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Three days IV. Then oral taper over twelve to eighteen months. ESR and CRP to monitor."
options.pt_remark_correct: (stands. picks up her bag unhurriedly.) This was not in the cards. I may need to recalibrate.

options.text: Discharge with oral prednisolone and GP follow-up
options.feedback_wrong: Vision-threatened GCA requires IV methylprednisolone inpatient, not oral prednisolone outpatient. Discharging at this stage risks further visual episodes without adequate steroid coverage.
options.pt_remark_wrong: For how long. I have clients on Thursday.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Transient visual loss this morning. Oral absorption is too slow."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Vision-threatened GCA discharged on oral steroids. She lost the eye two days later at home."

options.text: Discharge — she came alone and can make her own decisions
options.feedback_wrong: Respecting autonomy means ensuring informed decision-making — not abandoning a patient with an active vision-threatening emergency because she is difficult to engage. She agreed to stay.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (stands, picks up bag briskly) Good. I have a cat.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "She agreed to stay. You sent her anyway."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "She had agreed to stay. You discharged her. Autonomy does not mean abandonment."

options.text: Urgent outpatient rheumatology in one week
options.feedback_wrong: One week outpatient is not appropriate for active vision-threatened GCA requiring IV steroid initiation. This requires same-day inpatient admission.
options.pt_remark_wrong: One week. (quietly) A lot can happen in one week.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "She was right about that one."

report_template.pmh: hypertension, father with SAH from ruptured berry aneurysm
report_template.presenting: three weeks of progressive left-sided temporal headache, jaw claudication, two episodes of transient left visual loss

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: unilateral temporal headache progressive over three weeks, jaw pain on chewing, transient visual blurring x2 in past week, fatigue and malaise one month
pertinent_positives.pe: left temporal artery tender, nodular, markedly reduced pulsation; BP 172/96; remainder of examination not obtained
pertinent_positives.labs: ESR 98, CRP 64, normocytic anaemia Hgb 10.8, reactive thrombocytosis — marked systemic inflammation
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT head normal — no haemorrhage, no mass, no infarct
pertinent_positives.special: ESR 98 mm/hr, CRP 64 mg/L — markedly elevated

report_template.assessment: Giant Cell Arteritis with visual involvement — immediate treatment indicated
report_template.plan: IV methylprednisolone immediately (do not wait for biopsy), temporal artery biopsy within 2 weeks, inpatient admission, ophthalmology, rheumatology

explanation: Giant Cell Arteritis is a granulomatous large-vessel vasculitis affecting branches of the external carotid, particularly the temporal artery and ophthalmic artery. Age >50 (mean 72), female predominance. Classic features: new temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, tender/nodular/pulseless temporal artery, visual symptoms. ESR typically >50 (often >100). Risk of irreversible ischaemic optic neuropathy — permanent vision loss — if untreated. TREAT BEFORE BIOPSY in vision-threatened GCA.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Age >50, peak 70-80. Female predominance (3:1). Caucasian predominance. Associated with polymyalgia rheumatica (40-60%).
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Age, female sex, Northern European ancestry, PMR.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: New temporal headache (most common), jaw claudication (highly specific), scalp tenderness, tender/nodular/reduced-pulsation temporal artery, visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax → permanent loss), constitutional symptoms (fatigue, fever, weight loss).
explanation_detail.moa: Granulomatous inflammation of medium and large vessel walls — CD4+ T cells, macrophages, giant cells. Intimal hyperplasia → vessel occlusion → ischaemia. Ophthalmic artery involvement → ischaemic optic neuropathy.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Age >50, new temporal headache, jaw claudication, tender nodular pulseless temporal artery, ESR >50 (often >100), visual symptoms. TREAT BEFORE BIOPSY in vision-threatened disease. Biopsy valid 2 weeks post-steroids. Oral pred for uncomplicated, IV methylprednisolone for vision-threatened.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: ESR markedly elevated (>50, often >100). CRP elevated. Normocytic anaemia (inflammation). Thrombocytosis (reactive). Alkaline phosphatase may be elevated.
explanation_detail.treatment: Vision-threatened: IV methylprednisolone 500-1000mg x3 days then oral pred 1mg/kg. Uncomplicated: oral prednisolone 40-60mg. Taper slowly over 1-2 years. Temporal artery biopsy for confirmation (valid 2 weeks post-steroids). Aspirin 75mg (reduces ischaemic complications). Monitor ESR/CRP. PPI, bisphosphonate, calcium/vit D for steroid protection.

fennick_opening: "She rearranged the chair before sitting. I said nothing."
fennick_after_pe: "The artery. That is all she gave you. It was enough."
fennick_after_imaging: "No haemorrhage. The father's death was not her diagnosis."
cc_after_addl_hx: (has been touching her left temple and eye throughout) The gods do not send warnings without reason. This one has been the loudest.
cc_after_pe: (after temporal artery palpation — goes briefly quiet. then, briskly) Right. Yes. That is the one.
cc_after_imaging: The machine did not find my father's problem in me. Good. I told my daughter it would not.
cc_after_hint: You are getting warmer. I would tell you but then what would be the point of you.
cc_after_dx: (looks at you with something that might be satisfaction) I had a name for it too. Mine was older. But yes. Same thing.

title: The Postman's Foot

personality.archetype: ADHD postman. Enthusiastic. Tangential. Genuinely tries to answer but gets there via three other topics.
personality.voice: Sentences start with confidence and end somewhere else entirely. Not unintelligent — just parallel processing out loud. The cats are a constant.
personality.crack: When he realises the squatting is the cause. He goes quiet for exactly one beat. Then: 'Oh no. The cats.'

patient.name: The Postman
patient.occupation: Royal Mail Courier, Lower Gears — Ashgate route

pt_opening: Hello! Oh wow this place smells like old paper. I like it. Anyway —

chart.cc: My foot is doing something weird! Also I think I have a bad — oh that is a nice lamp, doc — uhh where was I. Right. The foot.
chart.hpi: The Postman presented in high spirits, having apparently stopped to greet two cats on the way in. He reports three weeks of progressive right foot weakness and an unusual gait — his foot drags when he walks and he has tripped on three separate occasions. It is his routine to squat and talk with cats he encounters on his route, sometimes for extended periods. He has no back pain. No preceding trauma. No recent illness. He takes methylphenidate for ADHD, prescribed.
chart.pmh: ADHD — on methylphenidate. No other conditions.
chart.meds: Methylphenidate (prescribed). Vitamins — unspecified, patient unsure if relevant.
chart.allergies: Penicillin — anaphylaxis. Details not elaborated on.
chart.fh: Father had 'something with his back.' Mother is fine. He has a fish called Gerald.
chart.sh: Royal Mail courier, Lower Gears to Ashgate route. On his feet six to eight hours daily. Non-smoker. Occasional ale on Fridays. Lives with a housemate and two cats (visiting).

additional_hx: On direct questioning: the foot weakness started gradually about three weeks ago, no single precipitating event he can identify. Pressed on activities that involve prolonged squatting or leg compression — he brightens considerably. He squats to talk to cats. Every cat on the route. He estimates he spends between forty minutes and two hours per shift in a deep squat position, knees fully flexed, chatting to local cats. He has done this for years. It is not new, he clarifies, but he has been making an extra effort recently because Mrs. Henderson's new kitten was shy. He does not see the relevance. He is asked to note the relevance.
patient_quote_addl_hx: I mean... I like talking to the cats. For hours sometimes. ... Is that relevant? They are very good listeners. Especially Mr. Fluffy — he does this thing where he tilts his head. Actually do you want to see a picture —
pe_findings: Right foot drop on walking — inability to dorsiflex the right foot against gravity. Eversion weakness. Inversion preserved. Sensory loss over the dorsum of the right foot and lateral lower leg. Sensation intact on the sole of the foot. Ankle reflex intact and equal bilaterally. Knee reflex intact. No lower back tenderness. No straight leg raise positivity. Tinel's sign positive at the fibular head on the right.
imaging: MRI right knee/fibular head: no mass lesion, no bony abnormality at the fibular head, no cystic lesion. Normal study. (Patient asked if the machine was taking his picture and whether he could see it.)

special_test.name: Nerve Conduction Study
special_test.result: Reduced conduction velocity and amplitude of the common fibular (peroneal) nerve at the fibular head. Tibial nerve conduction normal. L4/L5 nerve root conduction normal. Localised compression neuropathy confirmed at the fibular head.

memory_fragments.pe: He has been lifting his right knee higher for three weeks. He thought he was just walking enthusiastically. He told a kitten about it. The kitten was not concerned.
memory_fragments.imaging: The fibular head is where the nerve wraps around. There is nothing wrong with the bone. The bone did nothing. The squatting did everything.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Normal. WBC 7.1, Hgb 15.4, Plt 280.
tests.pt_remark: All normal? Okay cool. Hey does this mean my blood is good? Like, generally? I feel like it should come with a score.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Glucose 88, sodium 139, creatinine 0.9. LFTs normal.
tests.pt_remark: Normal again. You know what else is normal? Mr. Fluffy's vet results last month. We have that in common now.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. Not indicated for this presentation.
tests.pt_remark: Is that my heartbeat? That is so cool. Can I get a printout? For the fridge?

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. Not indicated for this presentation.
tests.pt_remark: My heart is fine though right? Because I did think about that. I asked around. People said various things.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.
tests.pt_remark: Is that the pancreas one? My housemate had pancreas trouble once. Very dramatic. Mine is fine though obviously.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.
tests.pt_remark: Normal. Great. Can we move on? I feel like we are running out of things to test and I still have a broken foot.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated for this presentation.
tests.pt_remark: Okay that one hurt a bit. I am not complaining. I am just noting it for the record.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.
tests.pt_remark: Also normal. You know what, I am starting to think the problem is specifically the foot.

hints: Right foot drop. Weakness on dorsiflexion and eversion. Inversion preserved. Sensory loss on the dorsum of the foot and lateral lower leg. Sensation on the sole intact. Ankle reflex normal. No back signs.
The sensory distribution matters. Dorsum of foot and lateral lower leg — not the sole. The sole is a different territory.
Tinel's at the fibular head. The nerve wraps around the neck of the fibula at that point. Prolonged external compression at that location produces a very specific pattern.
Forty minutes to two hours per shift in a deep squat. Knee fully flexed, fibular head compressed against whatever surface he is on. The nerve conduction localises it. The history explains it.

management.title: What structure is affected and at what level?
management.hint: Foot drop. Dorsiflexion and eversion weak. Inversion preserved. Sensory loss dorsum foot and lateral lower leg, sole intact. Tinel's at fibular head. Where is the lesion?

options.text: Common fibular nerve at the fibular head — compression neuropathy
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Common fibular nerve wraps around the fibular neck — uniquely vulnerable to compression here. Dorsiflexion (deep peroneal) and eversion (superficial peroneal) both affected because both branches arise distal to the compression. Inversion (tibial) and sole sensation (sural/tibial) preserved. Ankle reflex intact (tibial nerve). No back signs exclude L4/L5 root.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "The nerve turns the corner at the fibular head. That is where corners get cut."
options.pt_remark_correct: Oh! So it is like... a traffic jam. In my knee. For my foot. ... Oh no. ... The cats.

options.text: L4/L5 nerve root compression — disc herniation with foot drop
options.feedback_wrong: L4/L5 radiculopathy can cause foot drop but also causes back pain, positive straight leg raise, and altered knee or ankle reflexes depending on level. No back pain, no SLR positivity, normal reflexes. Nerve conduction localises to the fibular head, not the root.
options.pt_remark_wrong: My back is fine though! I have excellent posture. I deliver mail. I have thought about this.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "No back pain. No SLR. Normal reflexes. The root is not the problem."

options.text: Sciatic nerve palsy — proximal lesion affecting both peroneal and tibial divisions
options.feedback_wrong: Sciatic palsy affects both divisions — peroneal and tibial. Inversion and plantar flexion would also be weak, and sole sensation would be lost. His inversion is intact and sole sensation is normal. The tibial division is spared.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Is that the big one? The one that goes all the way up? That sounds much worse.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Sole sensation intact. Inversion intact. The tibial division is working fine."

options.text: Deep peroneal nerve palsy — isolated dorsiflexion weakness only
options.feedback_wrong: Deep peroneal palsy causes isolated dorsiflexion weakness and sensory loss only in the first web space. He also has eversion weakness and lateral lower leg sensory loss — indicating both branches are affected, which localises the lesion proximal to the bifurcation, at the common fibular nerve.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Is that different from what I have? Because my foot is still broken either way.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Eversion is also weak. That is the superficial branch. Both branches means the lesion is proximal to the split."

management.title: Nerve conduction confirms fibular head compression. What is the cause?
management.hint: 34-year-old postman. No trauma. No mass on MRI. Progressive over three weeks. The history has the answer.

options.text: Prolonged external compression from habitual deep squatting — occupational/behavioural cause
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Prolonged deep squatting compresses the common fibular nerve against the fibular head from the outside. Classic causes: habitual squatting, prolonged leg crossing, weight loss exposing the nerve, plaster casts. No trauma, no mass, no systemic disease — the cause is purely mechanical and modifiable.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "The nerve does not know it is being compressed by a cat conversation. It just stops conducting."
options.pt_remark_correct: Wait... so... the cats... did this to me? ... I mean. Not ON PURPOSE. They are cats. But. Oh no. Wait— so I just... I cannot squat anymore? ... Mr. Fluffy is going to think I am ignoring him.

options.text: Diabetic mononeuropathy — check HbA1c
options.feedback_wrong: Diabetic mononeuropathy is a vasculitic infarct of a single nerve — typically presents more acutely, often with pain, and in a diabetic patient. He has no diabetes history, normal CMP, and a clear mechanical cause on history. HbA1c is not indicated here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I do not have diabetes. I eat quite a lot of vegetables actually. Well. Sometimes.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Acute onset, painless, clear mechanical history. The cause is already in the room."

options.text: Vasculitic neuropathy — systemic inflammatory cause
options.feedback_wrong: Vasculitic neuropathy typically causes painful mononeuropathy multiplex — multiple nerves, painful, associated with systemic features. He has isolated painless mononeuropathy with a compelling mechanical explanation.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Vasculitis. That sounds bad. I Googled vasculitis once by accident. Very bad Google.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Painless. Single nerve. Clear history. Vasculitis does not hide in cat conversations."

options.text: Mass lesion at the fibular head — MRI excluded but consider ganglion cyst
options.feedback_wrong: MRI already excludes mass lesion. Ganglion cysts at the fibular head are a recognised cause but MRI would show them. The mechanical history with normal MRI makes positional compression the diagnosis.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A lump? I would have noticed a lump. I notice everything. Well. Most things. Well. The cats definitely.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "MRI is clear. The lump is not there. The squatting is."

management.title: Initial management
management.hint: Common fibular compression from habitual squatting. No structural cause. What is first-line?

options.text: Activity modification — cease prolonged squatting, padding at the fibular head, ankle foot orthosis (AFO) for foot drop, physiotherapy
options.feedback_correct: Correct. The cause is modifiable — stop the compression. AFO prevents tripping and maintains gait while the nerve recovers. Physiotherapy for strengthening and gait retraining. Most compressive fibular palsies recover fully with conservative management over weeks to months if the cause is removed.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Remove the cause. The nerve will do the rest. Nerves are patient."
options.pt_remark_correct: Wait... so I just... try harder at walking? ... That feels like motivational speech medicine. ... Also I cannot squat. ... Wait. You are telling me I cannot talk to my cat friends for hours anymore? ... No. ... Ms. Whiskers and Mr. Fluffy. ... They will miss me.

options.text: Surgical decompression at the fibular head — definitive treatment
options.feedback_wrong: Surgery is reserved for cases with structural compression (mass, cyst, bony abnormality) or failure of conservative management after adequate trial. No structural cause, no trauma, modifiable lifestyle cause — conservative management first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Surgery?! On my knee? I have a route tomorrow. Can they do it fast?
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "No structural cause. No failed conservative trial. The knife is not the first tool here."

options.text: High-dose oral corticosteroids — reduce nerve inflammation
options.feedback_wrong: Steroids are used for inflammatory neuropathies and vasculitic causes. This is mechanical compression — removing the compression source is the treatment. Steroids do not prevent ongoing compression.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Steroids? My housemate took steroids once for his back. He said it made him hungry. I am already quite hungry.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Mechanical compression is not inflammation. Removing the cause is."

options.text: EMG-guided botulinum toxin — relax spastic foot musculature
options.feedback_wrong: Botulinum toxin is used for spasticity — upper motor neurone lesions. This is a lower motor neurone/peripheral nerve lesion. Foot drop here is from weakness, not spasticity. Botox would worsen it.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Is that the one they use for faces? Because if so, absolutely not. I need my face.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Spastic foot drop is UMN. This foot is not spastic. It is weak. Different problem."

management.title: Disposition and follow-up
management.hint: Conservative management initiated. Nerve conduction confirms compressive neuropathy. Where does he go?

options.text: Outpatient physiotherapy referral, neurology or orthopaedics follow-up in 6-8 weeks, safety advice on tripping hazards, AFO fitted
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Stable, modifiable cause, no emergency. Outpatient physio. Follow-up in 6-8 weeks to reassess nerve recovery — if no improvement, repeat NCS and consider surgical review. Safety counselling important — foot drop is a significant fall risk especially on a postal route.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Nerves recover slowly. Weeks to months. Tell him not to count by cats."
options.pt_remark_correct: Okay. Okay. So. Six to eight weeks. I can do that. I will explain to Ms. Whiskers. She is very understanding. (thinks) Actually she mostly just stares. But I choose to read that as understanding. (stands up, immediately trips slightly on his own foot, catches himself) ... Still doing that. Yeah. Okay. Let's get that brace thing.

options.text: Admit for inpatient physiotherapy and monitoring
options.feedback_wrong: Stable compressive mononeuropathy with a clear modifiable cause does not require inpatient admission. Outpatient management is entirely appropriate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Admitted? Can I bring the cats? They are not technically mine but they know me.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Stable. Modifiable cause. A bed is not what the nerve needs."

options.text: Discharge with no follow-up — likely to resolve spontaneously
options.feedback_wrong: Follow-up is necessary to confirm recovery and identify the minority who do not improve and require surgical review. Discharge without follow-up misses the safety net.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Oh, just go? Okay! (already heading for the door) Wait do I get a note for the post office? Because my route efficiency has been terrible.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "If it does not recover, the next step is surgery. He needs a review point."

options.text: Urgent neurosurgery referral — foot drop requires surgical decompression
options.feedback_wrong: Urgent neurosurgery is not indicated for compressive mononeuropathy with a clear modifiable cause and no structural lesion. Conservative management first, surgical referral only if it fails.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Neurosurgery? That is the brain one, right? My foot is not in my brain. I checked.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "No structural cause. No failed conservative trial. Urgent surgery is not the level of response."

report_template.presenting: three weeks of progressive right foot drop and lateral leg numbness

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: habitual prolonged deep squatting while talking to cats on postal route, 40 mins to 2 hours per shift, three weeks duration
pertinent_positives.pe: right foot drop, dorsiflexion and eversion weakness, inversion intact, sensory loss dorsum foot and lateral lower leg, sole intact, ankle reflex normal, Tinel's at fibular head
pertinent_positives.imaging: MRI fibular head normal — no mass, no bony abnormality
pertinent_positives.special: nerve conduction study: reduced velocity and amplitude at fibular head, tibial nerve normal, L4/L5 root normal

report_template.assessment: Common fibular nerve compressive mononeuropathy at the fibular head, secondary to habitual prolonged squatting
report_template.plan: cease prolonged squatting, AFO for foot drop, physiotherapy, follow-up 6-8 weeks, safety advice re: tripping

explanation: Common fibular (peroneal) nerve wraps around the fibular neck — the most exposed peripheral nerve in the body. Compressive mononeuropathy here causes foot drop (dorsiflexion weakness), eversion weakness, and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral lower leg. Inversion and sole sensation preserved (tibial). Ankle reflex intact. Distinguished from L4/L5 radiculopathy by absence of back pain, normal SLR, normal reflexes, and NCS localisation. Treatment: remove cause, AFO, physio. Recovers in weeks to months.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Classically: prolonged squatting, leg crossing, weight loss (loss of protective fat pad), plaster cast pressure, trauma to fibular head. Also: ganglion cysts, tumours at fibular head.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Prolonged compression at fibular head — any cause. Thin patients (less protective tissue). Rapid weight loss.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Foot drop — can't dorsiflex, high-stepping gait. Eversion weak. Inversion intact (tibial). Sensory loss dorsum foot and lateral lower leg. Sole spared. Ankle reflex intact. Tinel's at fibular head.
explanation_detail.moa: Common fibular nerve wraps around the fibular neck just below the knee — subcutaneous, tethered, no protective muscle. External compression → demyelination → conduction block → weakness and sensory loss distal to lesion.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Foot drop + eversion weakness + intact inversion + dorsum sensory loss + intact sole + intact ankle reflex + Tinel's fibular head = common fibular. Distinguish from L4/L5 (back pain, SLR, reflex changes) and sciatic (tibial also affected). NCS localises.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: NCS: reduced velocity/amplitude at fibular head. MRI: exclude structural cause (ganglion, mass). EMG: denervation if axonal loss.
explanation_detail.treatment: Conservative: remove compression source, AFO, physiotherapy. Recovery weeks to months. Surgery if structural cause or failed conservative management. Botox NOT indicated (LMN, not UMN).

fennick_after_pe: "Dorsiflexion gone. Eversion gone. Inversion intact. Sole intact."
fennick_after_imaging: "No mass. No bone. The fibular head is clean."
generic_lab_remark: All normal? Okay. So it is definitely just the foot thing.
fennick_case_complete: "He tripped on the way out. Caught himself."
"He asked if the cats would understand. We will have to see about that."
cc_after_pe: Okay, I will follow your directions. Uhh. Actually I should not promise that.
cc_after_dx: Foot drop? Wait — so my foot is just... quitting its job? That is kind of dramatic actually.

title: The Beggar King's Humour

personality.archetype: Homeless man who believes himself a king. Slow, deliberate, regal. The dignity is entirely self-constructed and entirely sincere.
personality.voice: Archaic speech. Never hurries. Holds eye contact. The hands are dirty but the gestures are intentional. Every pause is theatrical.
personality.crack: When the neurological findings are mentioned. Half a second. Then it closes. He does not let it open again until the diagnosis.
personality.post_dx: Complete collapse of the persona. Fast, frightened, genuinely human. The king was always a performance. The fear underneath was always real.

patient.name: The Beggar King
patient.occupation: Sovereign of the Lower Gears Eastern Alley (self-proclaimed). No fixed abode.

chart.cc: A minor inconvenience has seen fit to disturb the royal schedule. ... Speak.
chart.hpi: The patient was brought in by a concerned ward constable who noted he had been behaving erratically in the Lower Gears district for several weeks — getting into altercations with local residents he refers to as his 'subjects,' slurring his speech at times, and on one occasion appearing confused about where he was. He denies any problem. He has been living without fixed abode for approximately two years. He is 33 years old. He has no history of significant alcohol use — constable confirms this. No head trauma. No recent illness. Family history of bipolar disorder in a first-degree relative.
chart.pmh: No known medical history. No prior psychiatric diagnoses.
chart.fh: First-degree relative with bipolar disorder — details not obtained.
chart.sh: No fixed abode, Lower Gears district. Non-drinker per constable report. Non-smoker. Diet described as 'whatever the kingdom provides.'

additional_hx: On direct questioning — conducted with considerable diplomatic negotiation — the following was established: mood has been more irritable than usual for approximately eight months, with episodes of rage disproportionate to provocation. Speech difficulties noted by others though patient denies them. Mild unsteadiness when walking, which he attributes to 'the weight of the crown.' Some difficulty with fine tasks he previously managed easily. He has not sought medical attention before. The constable notes that prior to approximately one year ago, the patient was considerably more lucid and less grandiose.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "My disposition is... vigorous. That is the quality of a king. It is the subjects who provoke it."
pe_findings: Alert, oriented to person but not reliably to place. Speech mildly dysarthric — more prominent on sustained phonation. Mild resting tremor of both hands, right greater than left — patient holds hands deliberately still when observed. Mild bradykinesia. Mild cogwheel rigidity on passive right arm movement. Gait mildly ataxic. Slit-lamp examination: slight discoloration at the periphery of both corneas — noted on careful inspection. Liver: mildly enlarged on palpation, non-tender. No asterixis.
imaging: CT head: mild generalised volume loss. No focal lesion, no haemorrhage. Findings consistent with early structural change — non-specific.

special_test.name: Serum Ceruloplasmin + 24hr Urine Copper + LFTs
special_test.result: Ceruloplasmin: 0.08 g/L (low — normal 0.20-0.60). 24-hour urine copper: 210 mcg/day (elevated — normal <100). AST 88, ALT 102 (elevated). Alkaline phosphatase 34 (low — characteristically low in Wilson disease). Serum copper: low (reflects low ceruloplasmin-bound copper). Free copper: elevated.

memory_fragments.pe: He has been holding his hands still for months. He noticed the tremor before anyone else did. He decided it was beneath a king to acknowledge it.
memory_fragments.imaging: The CT shows early change. It does not show what caused it. Whatever is doing this has been there for years. Long before the kingdom.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Mild haemolytic anaemia — Hgb 11.2, MCV 92 (normocytic), reticulocytes 3.2% (elevated). Coombs negative. Platelets 142 (mild thrombocytopenia).
tests.pt_remark: ... (glances at the result. looks away immediately.) ... The kingdom has weathered worse.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: AST 88, ALT 102 (elevated). ALP 34 (low). Bilirubin 28 (mildly elevated). Creatinine 0.9. Sodium 138. Glucose 91.
tests.pt_remark: (studying the wall) ... Liver. ... Interesting.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm rate 78. No arrhythmia.
tests.pt_remark: The heart is steady. ... As expected.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. Not indicated.
tests.pt_remark: The heart is fine. ... I said as much.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Proteinuria 1+ (mild). Aminoaciduria on microscopy. Consistent with renal tubular dysfunction.
tests.pt_remark: ... (the composure slips for exactly one second.) ... Protein. ... In the urine. ... (recovers) ... Proceed.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR 1.3 (mildly elevated). PTT 36. Consistent with mild hepatic synthetic dysfunction.
tests.pt_remark: (the hand shifts almost imperceptibly.) ... What does that mean for the blood.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.37. Normal. Not primarily indicated.
tests.pt_remark: Breathing is fine. ... Has always been fine.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.
tests.pt_remark: (waves a slow hand.) ... The pancreas is loyal.

hints: Psychiatric presentation — mood lability, irritability, personality change — in a 33-year-old with no alcohol use, no prior psychiatric history, and a family history of bipolar disorder. The family history is a trap. The liver enzymes are not.
AST and ALT elevated. ALP characteristically low. Mild haemolytic anaemia. Coombs negative. Proteinuria. Multiple organ systems involved — hepatic, neurological, renal, haematological.
Age under 40. Neurological symptoms plus liver disease plus haemolytic anaemia without an obvious cause. The corneal finding is subtle but present.
Ceruloplasmin low. 24-hour urine copper elevated. Free copper elevated. The copper has been accumulating since before the kingdom was established.

management.title: Genetic basis — which gene and inheritance pattern?
management.hint: Wilson disease confirmed biochemically. What is the underlying mutation?

options.text: ATP7B mutation — autosomal recessive
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Wilson disease is caused by mutations in ATP7B — encoding a copper-transporting ATPase in the liver. Autosomal recessive. Defective copper excretion into bile leads to hepatic accumulation, then overflow into brain, eyes, kidneys, and blood. Ceruloplasmin synthesis also impaired.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "ATP7B. The liver cannot move the copper out. So the copper moves itself — everywhere."
options.pt_remark_correct: I— ... I beg your pardon, physician. ... I did not— ... is it serious. ... Please. ... Tell me plainly.

options.text: ATP7A mutation — X-linked recessive
options.feedback_wrong: ATP7A mutations cause Menkes disease — X-linked recessive copper deficiency, not excess. Kinky hair, neurodegeneration in infancy. Opposite problem, different gene.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ... (the eyes shift.) ... That is not the right one.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Menkes is copper deficiency. Wilson is copper excess. Different gene, opposite direction."

options.text: HEXA mutation — autosomal recessive
options.feedback_wrong: HEXA mutations cause Tay-Sachs disease — hexosaminidase A deficiency, GM2 gangliosidosis. Not copper metabolism.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (slow blink.) ... That sounds like a different kingdom entirely.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Tay-Sachs. Not copper. Wrong metabolic pathway entirely."

options.text: HFE mutation — autosomal recessive
options.feedback_wrong: HFE mutations cause hereditary haemochromatosis — iron overload, not copper. Elevated ferritin, transferrin saturation. This patient has copper accumulation.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ... Iron. ... I could have been the iron king. ... (recovers) ... Apparently not.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Haemochromatosis is iron. The ceruloplasmin and urine copper already told you which metal."

management.title: First-line pharmacological treatment
management.hint: Wilson disease confirmed. Neurological and hepatic involvement. What is first-line treatment?

options.text: D-penicillamine
options.feedback_correct: Correct. D-penicillamine is a copper chelator — binds free copper and promotes urinary excretion. First-line for Wilson disease. Monitor for side effects: nephrotic syndrome, lupus-like reaction, bone marrow suppression. Zinc acetate is an alternative (blocks intestinal copper absorption) and is preferred in pre-symptomatic or pregnant patients.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Penicillamine binds what the liver cannot move. The kidneys finish the job."
options.pt_remark_correct: (voice drops completely) Please. ... Whatever this is. ... Give it to me. ... I will do whatever is required.

options.text: Phlebotomy
options.feedback_wrong: Phlebotomy reduces iron load — it is the treatment for haemochromatosis. It does not significantly reduce copper. Wilson disease requires chelation.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ... Blood removal. ... Is that what passes for medicine here.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Phlebotomy removes iron. The problem here is copper."

options.text: Corticosteroids
options.feedback_wrong: Wilson disease can mimic autoimmune hepatitis on biopsy and biochemistry — a common diagnostic trap. Steroids do not treat the underlying copper accumulation and would delay appropriate therapy.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (tilts head.) ... Inflammation. ... Is that what this is.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Wilson mimics autoimmune hepatitis. Steroids treat the mimic, not the metal."

options.text: Liver transplant
options.feedback_wrong: Liver transplant is curative in Wilson disease but reserved for acute liver failure or failed medical management. Medical therapy with chelation is first-line. Transplant is not the first step in a stable patient.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (goes very quiet.) ... The liver. ... (does not finish the sentence.)
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Transplant cures it. Chelation comes first. He is not in liver failure yet."

management.title: Multisystem involvement — which other organs are monitored?
management.hint: Wilson disease affects more than the liver and brain. What else needs monitoring?

options.text: Eyes, kidneys, bone marrow, bone
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Wilson disease is a multisystem copper deposition disorder. Eyes: Kayser-Fleischer rings (corneal copper deposition — slit lamp required, absent in ~50% of hepatic-only presentations). Kidneys: Fanconi syndrome — proximal tubular dysfunction causing aminoaciduria, phosphaturia, glucosuria. Haematological: Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia. Skeletal: osteoporosis, arthropathy. Cardiac: cardiomyopathy (rare).
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Copper deposits wherever the blood goes. Eyes, kidney tubules, bone marrow. Follow all of them."
options.pt_remark_correct: All of those places. ... I did not— ... physician, I am sorry. ... I should have come sooner. ... Can it still be fixed.

options.text: Lungs and thyroid only
options.feedback_wrong: Pulmonary and thyroid involvement is not characteristic of Wilson disease. The target organs are liver, brain, eyes, kidneys, blood, and bone.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ... The lungs work. ... The thyroid is not the concern.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Wilson does not target the lungs or thyroid. Follow the copper."

options.text: Cardiac monitoring only
options.feedback_wrong: Cardiac involvement occurs but is rare. Renal Fanconi syndrome, haemolytic anaemia, and Kayser-Fleischer rings are more consistent and important monitoring targets.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ... The heart is strong. ... Probably.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Cardiac is rare. Renal Fanconi and haemolytic anaemia are the consistent findings."

options.text: No additional monitoring beyond liver and brain
options.feedback_wrong: Wilson disease is explicitly multisystem. Fanconi syndrome (already evidenced by proteinuria and aminoaciduria here), haemolytic anaemia (already present), and Kayser-Fleischer rings are all present or likely in this patient.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ... If only.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "The proteinuria and haemolytic anaemia are already there. He has Fanconi. He has corneal changes. Both."

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Wilson disease confirmed, chelation initiated. Neurological and hepatic involvement, no acute failure. Where does he go?

options.text: Admit for initiation of chelation therapy, hepatology and neurology referral, social work involvement for housing and follow-up support
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Initiation of D-penicillamine requires monitoring for early side effects and baseline organ function assessment. Hepatology and neurology for ongoing management. Social work is essential — he has no fixed abode and will not maintain outpatient follow-up without support. Neurological symptoms may initially worsen with chelation (paradoxical worsening from mobilisation of copper).
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Chelation can worsen neurology initially. Start slowly. Watch closely."
options.pt_remark_correct: (very quietly) Yes. ... I will stay. ... However long you need. ... Please fix it. ... I am asking you.

options.text: Discharge with oral D-penicillamine prescription and outpatient hepatology in 4 weeks
options.feedback_wrong: No fixed abode. No support structure. D-penicillamine initiation requires monitoring and follow-up he cannot maintain without housing. Outpatient discharge here is not a realistic management plan.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (four weeks.) ... (very quietly.) ... I may not be findable in four weeks.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "No address. No follow-up possible. The prescription will not find him."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "No fixed abode. You discharged him with a prescription and a date. He did not make the appointment."

options.text: Refer to psychiatry for bipolar disorder — mood symptoms are the primary presentation
options.feedback_wrong: Mood symptoms in Wilson disease are secondary to copper deposition in the basal ganglia and limbic system. Treating as primary bipolar disorder misses the underlying cause. Chelation addresses the mood symptoms alongside the rest.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ... Psychiatry. ... You think this is madness.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "The basal ganglia have copper in them. That is why the mood changed. Treat the copper."

options.text: Urgent liver transplant assessment — Wilson disease is progressive without transplant
options.feedback_wrong: Transplant is reserved for acute liver failure or medical treatment failure. This patient has elevated transaminases and mild synthetic dysfunction — not acute failure. Chelation first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: (almost inaudible.) ... Right now. ... Is it that urgent.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Stable hepatic function. Chelation first. Transplant is the last resort, not the first step."

report_template.presenting: eight months of mood lability, irritability, dysarthria, tremor, and ataxia in a 33-year-old with no alcohol use and family history of bipolar disorder

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: progressive mood change and personality alteration over 8 months, speech difficulties, fine motor difficulty, unsteadiness
pertinent_positives.pe: dysarthria, bilateral resting tremor right greater than left, cogwheel rigidity, ataxic gait, mild hepatomegaly, subtle peripheral corneal discoloration
pertinent_positives.labs: elevated AST/ALT, low ALP, Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia, proteinuria with aminoaciduria, mildly elevated bilirubin

pertinent_positives.special: ceruloplasmin low 0.08, 24hr urine copper 210 mcg/day elevated, ALP characteristically low

report_template.assessment: Wilson Disease — ATP7B mutation, autosomal recessive, copper accumulation with hepatic, neurological, renal, and haematological involvement
report_template.plan: D-penicillamine chelation, inpatient initiation, hepatology and neurology referral, social work, monitor for paradoxical neurological worsening

explanation: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism caused by ATP7B mutations. Defective biliary copper excretion leads to hepatic accumulation, then overflow deposition in brain (basal ganglia, cerebellum), cornea (Kayser-Fleischer rings), kidneys, and blood. Presents before age 40. Neuropsychiatric presentation common — can mimic bipolar disorder, schizophrenia. Key labs: low ceruloplasmin, elevated 24hr urine copper, low ALP (characteristic), elevated transaminases. Treatment: D-penicillamine or trientine (chelation), zinc acetate (blocks absorption).

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Autosomal recessive. 1 in 30,000. Presents age 5-35. Hepatic presentation in children, neuropsychiatric more common in young adults.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Homozygous or compound heterozygous ATP7B mutation. Family history.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Hepatic: elevated transaminases, cirrhosis, acute liver failure (low ALP characteristic). Neurological: dysarthria, tremor, dystonia, ataxia, personality change. Psychiatric: mood lability, psychosis, mimics bipolar/schizophrenia. Eyes: Kayser-Fleischer rings (slit lamp). Renal: Fanconi syndrome. Blood: Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia.
explanation_detail.moa: ATP7B encodes a copper-transporting ATPase in hepatocytes. Mutation → impaired biliary copper excretion → hepatic copper accumulation → liver damage → copper overflow into circulation → deposition in brain, cornea, kidney tubules.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: ATP7B. Autosomal recessive. Age <40. Low ceruloplasmin. Elevated 24hr urine copper. Low ALP (characteristic). Kayser-Fleischer rings (slit lamp). Fanconi syndrome. Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia. D-penicillamine or trientine. Zinc acetate for pre-symptomatic/pregnant.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Ceruloplasmin low (<0.20). 24hr urine copper elevated (>100 mcg/day, >40 on chelation). Serum copper low (bound) but free copper high. ALP low (characteristic — seen in acute Wilson liver failure). LFTs elevated. Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia.
explanation_detail.treatment: D-penicillamine (copper chelator, first-line, monitor for nephrotic syndrome/lupus-like/bone marrow suppression). Trientine (alternative chelator, fewer side effects). Zinc acetate (blocks GI copper absorption — pre-symptomatic, maintenance, pregnancy). Liver transplant for acute liver failure or failed medical therapy. Paradoxical neurological worsening possible at chelation initiation — start low, go slow.

title: The Hangman's Complaint

personality.archetype: Enormous, masked executioner. Speaks in fragments. No emotion. Pre-dx: threatening undercurrent in everything. Post-dx: the threat drains out. Same short sentences — but honest now.
personality.voice: Subject. Verb. Stop. Never more than four words per sentence. Pre-dx every sentence has a quiet edge. Post-dx same structure but the edge is gone. Just a large man who is frightened.
personality.crack: When the RUQ is palpated. A sound. Not a word.

pt_opening: 나. 의사. 왔다. 몸. 이상하다. 고쳐라.

chart.cc: 나. 몸. 노랗다. 거울. 봤다. 괴물. 같다. 그리고. 춥다. 덜덜. 떨린다. 밧줄. 못. 묶겠다.
chart.hpi: The patient presented without appointment, occupying the doorway for several seconds before entering. He is a 40-year-old male state executioner who reports three days of progressive jaundice, rigors, and right upper quadrant pain. He noted his urine had darkened to the colour of rope. He had a similar but milder episode approximately four months ago that resolved without treatment. He declined to elaborate further.
chart.pmh: No known medical conditions. Similar biliary episode four months ago — self-resolved.
chart.sh: State executioner. Occupation physically demanding. Non-drinker. Diet unremarkable. Lives alone.

additional_hx: On direct questioning: the pain is localised to the right side under the ribs, sharp, worse after eating. The urine has been dark for three days, stools pale. He has had no appetite. The rigors started yesterday. He reports no chest pain, no vomiting blood, no change in bowel habit beyond pale stools. The previous episode resolved in two days. This one has not.
patient_quote_addl_hx: 오줌. 진하다. 밧줄. 색깔. 같다. 예전에도. 조금. 아팠다. 금방. 나았다. 이번엔. 다르다. 계속. 춥다.
pe_findings: Alert but uncomfortable. Markedly jaundiced sclera and skin. Febrile, diaphoretic. RUQ tenderness on palpation — patient made a sound and pulled back. Guarding present in RUQ. No peritoneal signs. Liver not enlarged. Spleen not palpable. Murphy's sign positive. No asterixis. No peripheral oedema.
imaging: Abdominal ultrasound: biliary ductal dilation. Common bile duct 12mm (dilated). Gallstones visible within the gallbladder. No free fluid. No hepatic mass.

memory_fragments.pe: He made a sound when the RUQ was palpated. Not a word. He had not made any sound before that.
memory_fragments.imaging: Dilated duct. Stone visible. The bile has nowhere to go. That is why he is yellow. That is why he has a fever. That is why he is here.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: WBC 18.4 (markedly elevated, left shift). Hgb 14.2. Plt 210. Leukocytosis consistent with bacterial infection.
tests.pt_remark: 피. 검사. 별로다.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Total bilirubin 6.8 (elevated — direct predominant). ALP 320 (elevated). GGT 280 (elevated). AST 88, ALT 96 (mildly elevated). Creatinine 1.4 (mildly elevated). Sodium 136.
tests.pt_remark: 숫자. 많다. 나쁜. 건가.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 114. No ischaemic changes.
tests.pt_remark: 심장. 빠르다. 알고. 있다.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. Not primarily indicated.
tests.pt_remark: 심장. 아니다. 나도. 안다.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Lipase 68 (normal upper limit 60 — borderline). Not significantly elevated. Pancreatitis not the primary diagnosis.
tests.pt_remark: 거기. 안. 아프다.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Bilirubin 3+ (elevated — conjugated bilirubin in urine). Urobilinogen low. Dark urine confirmed. No infection.
tests.pt_remark: 오줌. 얘기. 또. 하나.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR 1.6 (elevated). PTT 42. Consistent with hepatic dysfunction affecting synthetic function.
tests.pt_remark: 피. 잘. 안. 멈추나.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.32 (mild acidosis). Lactate 2.8 (elevated). Consistent with early sepsis physiology.
tests.pt_remark: 숨. 쉬기. 힘들다.

hints: Fever. RUQ pain. Jaundice. Three findings. One triad.
The bile duct is dilated on ultrasound. There is a stone. The duct is infected. Bile is not draining. The infection is building upstream.
Tachycardia. Fever. Elevated WBC with left shift. Mildly elevated creatinine. Lactate 2.8. This is not a stable patient.
The previous episode resolved in two days — likely a stone that passed. This one has not passed. The duct is obstructed. Decompression is eventually required.

management.title: Immediate resuscitation — first step
management.hint: Febrile, tachycardic, hypotensive, jaundiced, RUQ pain. What do you do first?

options.text: IV fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Acute cholangitis is a surgical emergency. IV fluids for haemodynamic support and broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately — before any further workup or intervention. Do not wait for blood cultures to return before starting antibiotics in a septic patient.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Obstruction plus infection plus systemic response. The order matters — fluids and antibiotics first."
options.pt_remark_correct: 주사. 맞는다. ... 아프면. 말해도. 되나.

options.text: Urgent ERCP immediately — decompress the biliary system first
options.feedback_wrong: ERCP is required but not the first step. The patient needs haemodynamic stabilisation and antibiotics before any procedure. ERCP on an unstable, non-resuscitated patient significantly increases procedural risk.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 수술. 지금. 바로. 하나.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Stabilise before you scope. The duct can wait one hour. The blood pressure cannot."

options.text: CT abdomen with contrast — confirm diagnosis before treating
options.feedback_wrong: The diagnosis is clinical — Charcot's triad with supporting ultrasound and labs. Delaying treatment for CT in a septic patient with confirmed biliary obstruction is not appropriate. Treat first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 또. 기계. 들어가나.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Charcot's triad. Dilated duct on ultrasound. The diagnosis is made. Treat it."

options.text: Observe and repeat bloods in four hours — may resolve spontaneously
options.feedback_wrong: This patient has sepsis physiology — tachycardia, hypotension, fever, elevated lactate. Watchful waiting in acute cholangitis with systemic sepsis is dangerous. The previous episode resolved because it was mild. This one has not.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 기다린다. 또.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Lactate 2.8. BP 96/62. HR 114. This is not a waiting case."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "He deteriorated within two hours. Reynolds Pentad by morning."

management.title: Antibiotic selection
management.hint: Broad-spectrum antibiotics for biliary sepsis. The source is the biliary tree. What covers it?

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam (pip-tazo) — broad Gram-negative and anaerobic coverage
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Pip-tazo provides excellent coverage for the common biliary pathogens — E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, and anaerobes. First-line for moderate-severe acute cholangitis per Tokyo Guidelines. Alternatives: carbapenems for severe/resistant cases, ceftriaxone + metronidazole for mild-moderate.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Gram-negatives from the gut. Enterococcus. Anaerobes. Pip-tazo covers all three lanes."
options.pt_remark_correct: 약. 독한. 건가. ... 괜찮다. 맞겠다.

options.text: Vancomycin monotherapy — cover Gram-positive organisms
options.feedback_wrong: Biliary infections are predominantly Gram-negative — E. coli, Klebsiella. Vancomycin monotherapy leaves the dominant pathogens uncovered.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 약. 이름. 모른다.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "The biliary tree grows Gram-negatives. Vancomycin does not reach them."

options.text: Fluconazole — cover Candida biliary infection
options.feedback_wrong: Fungal cholangitis is rare and reserved for immunocompromised patients with failed bacterial therapy. This is bacterial cholangitis — start with bacterial coverage.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 곰팡이. 아니다. 나는.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Fungal cholangitis is immunocompromised patients only. Start with bacteria."

options.text: Metronidazole monotherapy — anaerobic biliary coverage
options.feedback_wrong: Metronidazole covers anaerobes but not the dominant Gram-negative aerobes in biliary sepsis. Monotherapy is insufficient.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 약. 하나만. 맞는다.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Anaerobic coverage alone misses E. coli and Klebsiella. The dominant organisms here."

management.title: ERCP timing — when to decompress
management.hint: Antibiotics started, fluids running. Patient remains febrile and tachycardic. When does ERCP happen?

options.text: Urgent ERCP within 24-48 hours — after initial stabilisation with antibiotics and fluids
options.feedback_correct: Correct. For moderate-severe cholangitis without Reynolds Pentad, ERCP within 24-48 hours after initial stabilisation is the standard. Antibiotics buy time and reduce septic load — ERCP then removes the obstruction definitively. Early ERCP within 24h is preferred for Tokyo Grade II/III.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Antibiotics treat the infection. ERCP removes the cause. Both are required. In that order."
options.pt_remark_correct: 카메라. 목구멍. 넣는다. ... 싫다. 그래도. 한다.

options.text: ERCP only if fever persists beyond 72 hours of antibiotics
options.feedback_wrong: Waiting 72 hours in obstructive cholangitis risks further deterioration and progression to Reynolds Pentad. Antibiotics alone cannot clear an obstructed duct. ERCP within 24-48 hours is the standard.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 3일. 기다린다. 알겠다.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Antibiotics suppress the infection. They do not unblock the duct. The obstruction remains."

options.text: Proceed immediately with ERCP before further antibiotic doses
options.feedback_wrong: ERCP without adequate stabilisation carries higher procedural risk. The patient is currently hypotensive and tachycardic. A minimum period of resuscitation improves outcomes before endoscopy.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 지금. 바로. 하나.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "BP 96/62. Resuscitate first. The endoscope can wait one hour."

options.text: Elective ERCP in 2 weeks after discharge — once infection clears
options.feedback_wrong: Elective outpatient ERCP is for incidental bile duct stones in stable patients, not for acute cholangitis with systemic sepsis. This patient needs inpatient biliary decompression.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 2주. 후. 퇴원. 가능. 한가.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Obstructed infected bile duct does not wait two weeks. Neither does sepsis."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "He deteriorated overnight. Emergency ERCP at 3am. The delay cost him."

management.title: Reynolds Pentad — what triggers urgent ERCP?
management.hint: The patient suddenly becomes confused and his blood pressure drops further. Which combination mandates emergency ERCP within hours?

options.text: Charcot's Triad plus hypotension and altered mental status
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Reynolds Pentad = Charcot's Triad (RUQ pain + fever + jaundice) plus hypotension and altered mental status. This represents suppurative cholangitis — a life-threatening emergency requiring emergency biliary decompression within hours, not 24-48 hours. Mortality without urgent drainage is very high.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Five findings. Not three. When the mind goes and the pressure drops — hours, not days."

options.text: Charcot's Triad plus bloody diarrhoea and peritoneal signs
options.feedback_wrong: Bloody diarrhoea and peritoneal signs suggest a different abdominal emergency — ischaemic colitis, perforation. They are not part of Reynolds Pentad. The Pentad specifically adds hypotension and altered mental status to Charcot's Triad.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 피똥. 안. 쌌다. 나는.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Reynolds adds hemodynamic collapse and CNS dysfunction. Not GI bleeding."

options.text: Charcot's Triad plus elevated troponin and new ST changes
options.feedback_wrong: Cardiac findings indicate a separate pathology — demand ischaemia from sepsis is possible but troponin elevation and ST changes are not part of Reynolds Pentad and do not independently trigger emergency ERCP.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 심장. 얘기. 또. 나온다.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Troponin can rise in sepsis. It does not change the Pentad."

options.text: Charcot's Triad plus WBC above 20 and lactate above 4
options.feedback_wrong: Severely elevated WBC and lactate indicate sepsis severity and should prompt aggressive management — but they are not the defined components of Reynolds Pentad. The clinical findings of hypotension and altered consciousness define the Pentad.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 숫자. 또. 나쁜. 건가.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Reynolds is clinical — blood pressure and mental status. Not laboratory values."

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Cholangitis confirmed. Antibiotics started, fluids running, ERCP planned. Where does he go?

options.text: Admit to monitored bed — gastroenterology and general surgery, ERCP within 24-48 hours
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Acute cholangitis with sepsis physiology requires inpatient admission with close monitoring. GI for ERCP, surgery on standby if ERCP fails. NPO in preparation for procedure. Serial vitals and lactate to monitor response to antibiotics.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "NPO. Monitored bed. ERCP team informed. Surgery aware."
options.pt_remark_correct: 입원. 한다. ... 방. 혼자. 쓰나. (after a long pause) ... 배. 아프면. 불러도. 되나.

options.text: Discharge with oral antibiotics and outpatient gastroenterology referral
options.feedback_wrong: This patient has sepsis, hypotension, and biliary obstruction requiring IV antibiotics and ERCP. Oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-up are not appropriate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 집에. 간다. 알겠다.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Hypotension. IV antibiotics. ERCP required. He cannot go home tonight."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "He went home. He came back by ambulance six hours later."

options.text: Admit to ICU — septic shock requires intensive care
options.feedback_wrong: ICU is appropriate if Reynolds Pentad develops or the patient fails to respond to initial resuscitation. Currently he is septic but not in refractory shock — a monitored bed with close observation is appropriate. Escalate to ICU if he deteriorates.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 중환자실. 무겁다. 그. 단어.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Monitored ward first. ICU if he does not respond. Do not skip steps."

options.text: Emergency surgery — cholecystectomy tonight
options.feedback_wrong: Cholecystectomy is definitive treatment for gallstone disease but is performed electively after the acute episode resolves — not during active cholangitis. ERCP first to decompress, surgery later.
options.pt_remark_wrong: 수술. 칼. 싫다.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Decompress first with ERCP. Cholecystectomy is for after the inflammation settles."

report_template.presenting: three days of jaundice, rigors, and right upper quadrant pain with haemodynamic compromise

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: dark urine, pale stools, prior similar episode, no appetite, rigors since yesterday
pertinent_positives.pe: jaundice, fever, RUQ tenderness with guarding, positive Murphy's sign, tachycardia, hypotension
pertinent_positives.labs: WBC 18.4 left shift, bilirubin 6.8 direct-predominant, ALP 320, GGT 280, INR 1.6, lactate 2.8, creatinine 1.4

report_template.assessment: Acute Cholangitis (Charcot's Triad) with sepsis — biliary obstruction from choledocholithiasis
report_template.plan: IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics (pip-tazo), ERCP within 24-48 hours, monitored admission, GI and surgery referral

explanation: Acute cholangitis results from bacterial infection of an obstructed biliary system — most commonly from choledocholithiasis. Charcot's Triad: RUQ pain + fever/rigors + jaundice. Reynolds Pentad adds hypotension and altered mental status — indicates suppurative cholangitis, a life-threatening emergency. Treatment: IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics (pip-tazo or carbapenem), biliary decompression via ERCP (24-48h for moderate, emergency for Reynolds Pentad). Cholecystectomy electively after resolution.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Gallstones most common cause. Also: biliary strictures, malignancy, PSC, parasites. Any age.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Cholelithiasis, prior biliary procedures, PSC, malignant biliary obstruction.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Charcot's Triad in ~50-70%. RUQ pain + fever + jaundice. Dark urine, pale stools. Reynolds Pentad (adds hypotension + AMS) = suppurative cholangitis, life-threatening.
explanation_detail.moa: Biliary obstruction → bile stasis → bacterial overgrowth (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, anaerobes) → ascending infection → bacteraemia → sepsis. INR elevation from hepatic synthetic dysfunction.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Charcot's Triad = RUQ pain + fever + jaundice. Reynolds Pentad = + hypotension + AMS. Dilated CBD on US. Elevated ALP/GGT/bilirubin (direct). Pip-tazo first line. ERCP to decompress. Cholecystectomy after. Tokyo Guidelines grade severity.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: WBC elevated with left shift. Direct hyperbilirubinaemia. ALP and GGT elevated (obstructive pattern). AST/ALT mildly elevated. INR elevated (synthetic dysfunction). Lactate elevated if sepsis. UA: bilirubin positive, urobilinogen low.
explanation_detail.treatment: Mild: antibiotics + elective ERCP. Moderate/severe: IV antibiotics + ERCP 24-48h. Reynolds Pentad: emergency ERCP within hours. Antibiotics: pip-tazo (first line), carbapenems (severe/resistant), ceftriaxone + metronidazole (mild-moderate). Cholecystectomy electively after resolution.
